output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.95}}

Summary: Critique focused on unverifiable citations, internal numerical/logical tensions, and framing gaps; recommendations below.

**Citations look fabricated or unverifiable.** Every name-cited prior work in paragraph 1 is referenced by surname-only with no year, venue, or specific claim attribution — "Wang et al.," "Kim and Park," "Ramirez et al.," "Chen et al." This is the pattern of placeholder or hallucinated citations. Before submission, each one must be replaced with a specific, verifiable reference (author, year, venue, title), and the attributed claim must actually appear in that work. "SemWER" in particular reads like an invented term; if it is a real proposed metric, cite the exact paper. If it is your own coinage, do not attribute it to "Kim and Park." Real foundational references in this area that probably belong here include Morris et al. 2004 (WER critique), the semantic distance / embedding-based WER work (e.g., Kim et al. on semdist), and entity-WER work tied to NER-aware scoring (e.g., the NVIDIA / Google entity-WER threads) — verify before citing.

**The headline number "2.4×" lacks a definition.** "Underestimates the impact ... by a factor of roughly 2.4× relative to their downstream cost on intent accuracy" is not a well-formed ratio as written. Underestimates *what quantity* by 2.4×? Define the numerator and denominator (e.g., "WER attributes X% of total error mass to code-switch boundaries, whereas a downstream-cost attribution assigns 2.4·X%"). Otherwise readers cannot reproduce or sanity-check the claim.

**Rank-disagreement claim is under-specified.** "Disagreed ... on 4 of the 6 systems" is ambiguous — pairwise rank disagreement is normally reported as Kendall's τ or as the number of inverted pairs out of (6 choose 2)=15. "4 of 6 systems" suggests per-system position changes, which is a weaker and less standard statistic. Report τ or Spearman ρ alongside, and state the comparison set explicitly.

**r = 0.91 vs 0.62 needs a confidence interval and a leakage disclosure.** Since the composite metric's weights are *fit* to downstream task cost, the 0.91 correlation is in-sample unless you held out systems/utterances. State explicitly whether 0.91 is cross-validated, held-out, or in-sample fit; without that, the comparison to 0.62 is not apples-to-apples. Also report n and a CI.

**Dataset composition has an internal tension.** You describe the corpus as covering "conversational, accented, and code-switched speech" and list CommonVoice, Switchboard-Cellular, and CHiME-7. CommonVoice is read speech, not conversational; Switchboard-Cellular is conversational but English-only and dated; CHiME-7 is far-field/noisy. Either revise the descriptor ("read accented, conversational, far-field, and code-switched") or justify why CommonVoice is being used as a conversational source. Also: "Switchboard-Cellular" — verify the exact corpus name (Switchboard Cellular Part 1/2, LDC2001S13/LDC2004S07) and cite the LDC catalog ID.

**"Six state-of-the-art open ASR systems" is unsubstantiated as stated.** Name them, or at least specify the selection criterion and date of the snapshot. "State-of-the-art" without a leaderboard reference is hand-waving and will draw a reviewer flag.

**Taxonomy of nine categories appears without justification.** Where does the nine-category taxonomy come from — derived in this work, adopted from prior work, or annotator-defined? The fine-tuned classifier's accuracy on this taxonomy needs to be reported, because all downstream weight-fitting depends on its labels.

**Contribution framing is reasonable but slightly oversells novelty.** Downstream-task-grounded ASR evaluation is not new (see e.g., He et al. on task-oriented ASR evaluation, and the long line of "WER is not all you need" papers). The genuine delta here appears to be (a) the *calibrated weights* per error category, and (b) the code-switched evaluation suite. Sharpen the final paragraph to claim those two specifically, rather than the more diffuse "downstream-grounded composite metric," which has prior art.

**Minor prose issues:**
- "comprised 142 hours ... drawn from three ... datasets ... augmented with 18 hours" — "comprised of" is wrong, but "comprised" used transitively here is fine; just verify the totals (142 + 18 = 160 h?) and state the combined figure.
- "fine-tuned classifier" — fine-tuned from what base model? One clause needed.
- "frozen NLU model" — name it; otherwise the intent-accuracy number is not reproducible.
- "intent-preserving reference transcripts" — define how these differ from verbatim; the distinction is doing real work in your weighting argument.

Uncertainty: I have not verified that the four cited author groups (Wang, Kim & Park, Ramirez, Chen) correspond to real papers in this area — my strong suspicion is that at least some are placeholders or fabrications, but I did not run a literature lookup. The LDC catalog IDs for Switchboard Cellular cited above are from memory and should be confirmed.
